title: The statement of cash flows: operating, investing, financing

questions.explanation: Under US GAAP, interest paid (and interest/dividends received) is operating because it flows through the income statement. Only loan principal moves through financing.

Depreciation moves no cash when recorded — the cash left in investing when the asset was bought. The indirect method adds it back to net income to undo the non-cash expense; it is never itself a cash flow.

Asset sales appear at full proceeds ($52,000) in investing. The $12,000 gain sits inside net income, so the indirect method subtracts it in operating to avoid double-counting. The $40,000 book value is not a cash flow at all.
CFO +, CFI −, CFF − is the mature-firm signature: operations generate cash, some is reinvested, and the surplus is returned to lenders and shareholders (dividends, buybacks, debt paydown).
CFO = 75,000 NI + 20,000 depreciation + 3,000 loss added back − 9,000 AR increase + 4,000 inventory decrease + 6,000 AP increase = 99,000.
FCF = CFO − capex = 120,000 − 70,000 = 50,000. The van proceeds are an investing inflow, not a capex reduction in the baseline definition, and dividends are a financing outflow — neither enters FCF.

Operating: 240,000 − 195,000 − 5,000 interest = +40,000. Investing: −50,000 capex + 8,000 proceeds = −42,000. Financing: +20,000 borrowing − 12,000 dividends = +8,000. Net change +6,000; ending cash = 30,000 + 6,000 = 36,000.
The indirect method starts from net income and adjusts for non-cash items, gains/losses, and working-capital changes to arrive at CFO.
